title.zh: 卷九十四下 匈奴傳
title.raw: 卷九十四下 匈奴傳 Volume 94b: Traditions of the Xiongnu 2

sentences.zh: 卷九十四下 匈奴傳第六十四下

translations.literal: Volume 94b below: Traditions of the Xiongnu, the sixty-fourth below.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 94b: Traditions of the Xiongnu (continued).

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪單于歸庭數月，罷兵使各歸故地，乃收其兄呼屠吾斯在民間者立為左谷蠡王，使人告右賢貴人，欲令殺右賢王。

translations.literal: Several months after Chanyu Huhanye returned to the court, he dismissed the armies and sent each to return to former lands; then he gathered his elder brother Hutuwusi who was among the common people and established him as Left Gu-li King, sent men to tell the Right Virtuous nobles, wishing to have them kill the Right Virtuous King.
translations.idiomatic: A few months after Chanyu Huhanye had re-established his court, he sent the troops home to their old pastures. He then located his brother Hutuwusi, who had been living among the people, and installed him as Left Gu-li King, and he instructed the nobility of the Right Virtuous King to put that king to death.

sentences.zh: 其冬，都隆奇與右賢王共立日逐王薄胥堂為屠耆單于，發兵數萬人東襲呼韓邪單于。

translations.literal: That winter, Dou Longqi together with the Right Virtuous King jointly established Rizhu Wang Boxutang as Chanyu Tuqi, raised troops numbering tens of thousands and struck east against Chanyu Huhanye.
translations.idiomatic: That winter Dou Longqi and the Right Virtuous King raised the Rizhu king Boxutang to the throne as Chanyu Tuqi. They mobilized tens of thousands of riders and marched east to attack Chanyu Huhanye.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪單于兵敗走，屠耆單于還，以其長子都塗吾西為左谷蠡王，少子姑瞀樓頭為右谷蠡王，留居單于庭。

translations.literal: Chanyu Huhanye’s troops were defeated and fled; Chanyu Tuqi returned, appointing his eldest son Dutuwuxi as Left Gu-li King and his younger son Gumouloutou as Right Gu-li King, and remained residing at the Chanyu court.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye’s forces were routed and withdrew. Tuqi then returned, named his eldest son Dutuwuxi Left Gu-li King and his younger son Gumouloutou Right Gu-li King, and took up residence again at the Chanyu headquarters.

sentences.zh: 明年秋，屠耆單于使日逐王先賢撣兄右奧鞬王為烏藉都尉各二萬騎，屯東方以備呼韓邪單于。

translations.literal: The next autumn, Chanyu Tuqi ordered Rizhu Wang Xianxiandan and Xianxiandan’s elder brother the Right Ao-jian Wang each to serve as Wuji Commandant with twenty thousand riders, encamped in the east to guard against Chanyu Huhanye.
translations.idiomatic: The following autumn Tuqi placed both the Rizhu king Xianxiandan and Xianxiandan’s elder brother, the Right Ao-jian king, in the post of Wuji commandant, each commanding twenty thousand riders posted eastward to block Huhanye.

sentences.zh: 是時，西方呼揭王來與唯犁當戶謀，共讒右賢王，言欲自立為烏藉單于。

translations.literal: At this time, from the west King Hujie came and with Weili Danghu plotted, together slandering the Right Virtuous King, saying he wished to establish himself as Chanyu Wuji.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile the western king Hujie joined the household chief Weili Danghu in denouncing the Right Virtuous King, claiming he intended to declare himself Chanyu Wuji.

sentences.zh: 屠耆單于殺右賢王父子，後知其冤，復殺唯犁當戶。

translations.literal: Chanyu Tuqi killed the Right Virtuous King father and son; afterward knowing their injustice, he again killed Weili Danghu.
translations.idiomatic: Tuqi executed the Right Virtuous King and his son; when he realized they had been wronged, he put Weili Danghu to death as well.

sentences.zh: 於是呼揭王恐，遂畔去，自立為呼揭單于。

translations.literal: Thereupon King Hujie feared, then rebelled and left, establishing himself as Chanyu Hujie.
translations.idiomatic: Hujie panicked, broke away, and proclaimed himself Chanyu Hujie.

sentences.zh: 右奧鞬王聞之，即自立為車犁單于。

translations.literal: The Right Ao-jian Wang hearing it, immediately established himself as Chanyu Cheli.
translations.idiomatic: The Right Ao-jian king heard this and at once declared himself Chanyu Cheli.

sentences.zh: 烏藉都尉亦自立為烏藉單于。

translations.literal: The Wuji Commandant also established himself as Chanyu Wuji.
translations.idiomatic: The Wuji commandant likewise raised his own banner as Chanyu Wuji.

sentences.zh: 凡五單于。

translations.literal: In all there were five Chanyus.
translations.idiomatic: There were now five rival Chanyus.

sentences.zh: 屠耆單于自將兵東擊車犁單于，使都隆奇擊烏藉。

translations.literal: Chanyu Tuqi personally led troops east to strike Chanyu Cheli, ordering Dou Longqi to strike Wuji.
translations.idiomatic: Tuqi took the field himself against Cheli in the east while sending Dou Longqi to attack Wuji.

sentences.zh: 烏藉、車犁皆敗，西北走，與呼揭單于兵合為四萬人。

translations.literal: Wuji and Cheli both were defeated, fled northwest, and joined with Chanyu Hujie’s troops to become forty thousand men.
translations.idiomatic: Wuji and Cheli were beaten and fled northwest; they merged with Hujie’s forces into an army of forty thousand.

sentences.zh: 烏藉、呼揭皆去單于號，共並力尊輔車犁單于。

translations.literal: Wuji and Hujie both discarded the Chanyu title, together combining strength to honor and assist Chanyu Cheli.
translations.idiomatic: Wuji and Hujie dropped their Chanyu titles and united behind Cheli as their sole leader.

sentences.zh: 屠耆單于聞之，使左大將、都尉將四萬騎分屯東方，以備呼韓邪單于，自將四萬騎西擊車犁單于。

translations.literal: Chanyu Tuqi hearing it, ordered the Left Great General and Commandant to lead forty thousand riders divided to encamp in the east, to guard against Chanyu Huhanye; he personally led forty thousand riders west to strike Chanyu Cheli.
translations.idiomatic: Tuqi detached forty thousand riders under his Left Great General and commandants to hold the east against Huhanye, while he personally marched west with another forty thousand against Cheli.

sentences.zh: 車犁單于敗，西北走，屠耆單于即引西南，留闟敦地。

translations.literal: Chanyu Cheli was defeated, fled northwest; Chanyu Tuqi then led southwest, staying in Tidun territory.
translations.idiomatic: Cheli was routed and withdrew northwest; Tuqi swung southwest and halted in the Tidun region.

sentences.zh: 其明年，呼韓邪單于遣其弟右谷蠡王等西襲屠耆單于屯兵，殺略萬餘人。

translations.literal: The next year, Chanyu Huhanye sent his younger brother the Right Gu-li King and others west to raid Tuqi’s encamped troops, killing and plundering over ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: The year after, Huhanye sent his brother the Right Gu-li king and other commanders west against Tuqi’s camp, killing or carrying off more than ten thousand people.

sentences.zh: 屠耆單于聞之，即自將六萬騎擊呼韓邪單于，行千里，未至嗕姑地，逢呼韓邪單于兵可四萬人，合戰。

translations.literal: Chanyu Tuqi hearing it, immediately personally led sixty thousand riders to strike Chanyu Huhanye, traveling a thousand li; before reaching Rugudi, he encountered Chanyu Huhanye’s troops of about forty thousand, and they joined battle.
translations.idiomatic: Tuqi rode out at the head of sixty thousand horsemen and marched a thousand li to meet Huhanye. Before he reached Rugu he ran into some forty thousand of Huhanye’s men, and the two sides fought.

sentences.zh: 屠耆單于兵敗，自殺。

translations.literal: Chanyu Tuqi’s troops were defeated; he killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Tuqi’s army was broken and he took his own life.

sentences.zh: 都隆奇乃與屠耆少子右谷蠡王姑瞀樓頭亡歸漢，車犁單于東降呼韓邪單于。

translations.literal: Dou Longqi then together with Tuqi’s younger son the Right Gu-li King Gumouloutou fled and submitted to Han; Chanyu Cheli east submitted to Chanyu Huhanye.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Longqi fled to Han with Tuqi’s younger son Gumouloutou, still Right Gu-li king, while Cheli submitted to Huhanye in the east.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪單于左大將烏厲屈與父呼速累烏厲溫敦皆見匈奴亂，率其眾數萬人南降漢。

translations.literal: Chanyu Huhanye’s Left Great General Wuli Qu and father Husulei Wuli Wendun both seeing the Xiongnu in chaos, led their multitudes of tens of thousands south to surrender to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye’s Left Great General Wuli Qu and his father Wuli Wendun of the Husulei clan, seeing the realm in turmoil, brought tens of thousands of followers south to defect to the Han.

sentences.zh: 封烏厲屈為新城侯，烏厲溫敦為義陽侯。

translations.literal: Wuli Qu was enfeoffed as Marquis of Xincheng, Wuli Wendun as Marquis of Yiyang.
translations.idiomatic: The court made Wuli Qu marquis of Xincheng and Wuli Wendun marquis of Yiyang.

sentences.zh: 是時，李陵子復立烏藉都尉為單于，呼韓邪單于捕斬之，遂復都單于庭，然眾裁數萬人。

translations.literal: At this time, Li Ling’s son again established the Wuji Commandant as Chanyu; Chanyu Huhanye captured and beheaded him, then again established capital at the Chanyu court, yet the multitude was only several tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: Then the son of Li Ling set up the Wuji commandant as a rival Chanyu; Huhanye seized and executed him and reclaimed the royal headquarters, though his following numbered only a few tens of thousands.

sentences.zh: 屠耆單于從弟休旬王將所主五六百騎，擊殺左大且渠，並其兵，至右地，自立為閏振單于，在西邊。

translations.literal: Tuqi’s cousin King Xiuxun led the five or six hundred riders he commanded, attacked and killed the Left Great Juqu, annexed his troops, reached the right territory, established himself as Chanyu Runzhen, on the western side.
translations.idiomatic: Tuqi’s cousin, the Xiuxun king, led his five or six hundred horsemen against the Left Great Juqu, absorbed that force, marched into the western steppe, and proclaimed himself Chanyu Runzhen.

sentences.zh: 其後，呼韓邪單于兄左賢王呼屠吾斯亦自立為郅支骨都侯單于，在東邊。

translations.literal: Afterward, Chanyu Huhanye’s elder brother the Left Virtuous King Hutuwusi also established himself as Chanyu Zhizhi Gutuhou, on the eastern side.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward Huhanye’s elder brother Hutuwusi, the Left Virtuous king, also crowned himself Chanyu Zhizhi Gutuhou and held the eastern wing.

sentences.zh: 其後二年，閏振單于率其眾東擊郅支單于。

translations.literal: Two years after that, Chanyu Runzhen led his multitude east to strike Chanyu Zhizhi.
translations.idiomatic: Two years later Runzhen marched east against Zhizhi.

sentences.zh: 郅支單于與戰，殺之，並其兵，遂進攻呼韓邪。

translations.literal: Chanyu Zhizhi fought with him, killed him, annexed his troops, then advanced to attack Huhanye.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi met him in battle, slew him, absorbed his army, and pressed on against Huhanye.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪破，其兵走，郅支都單于庭。

translations.literal: Huhanye was broken, his troops fled; Zhizhi established capital at the Chanyu court.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye was routed and his men scattered; Zhizhi seized the royal seat at the Chanyu court.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪之敗也，左伊秩訾王為呼韓邪計，勸令稱臣入朝事漢，從漢求助，如此匈奴乃定。

translations.literal: When Huhanye was defeated, the Left Yizhi King planned for Huhanye, urging him to declare himself subject, enter court and serve Han, follow Han to seek help; thus the Xiongnu would be stabilized.
translations.idiomatic: After Huhanye’s defeat the Left Yizhi king urged him to acknowledge Han suzerainty, attend the imperial court, and borrow Han strength; only then, he argued, could the Xiongnu regain peace.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪議問諸大臣，皆曰：「不可。

translations.literal: Huhanye deliberated and questioned the great ministers; all said: ‘It cannot be done.’
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye put the idea before his nobles, who replied with one voice: ‘No.’

sentences.zh: 匈奴之俗，本上氣力而下服役，以馬上戰鬥為國，故有威名於百蠻。

translations.literal: The custom of the Xiongnu fundamentally esteems strength and despises servitude; taking combat on horseback as the state, therefore they have awe-inspiring name among the hundred barbarians.
translations.idiomatic: Among our people valor ranks highest and subservience is despised; we are a nation born in the saddle, and that is why the hundred tribes fear our name.

sentences.zh: 戰死，壯士所有也。

translations.literal: To die in battle is what stalwart men possess.
translations.idiomatic: To fall in battle is the warrior’s portion.

sentences.zh: 今兄弟爭國，不在兄則在弟，雖死猶有威名，子孫常長諸國。

translations.literal: Now brothers contend for the state; if not the elder then the younger; though dead still there is awe-inspiring name; descendants constantly lord over the states.
translations.idiomatic: Brothers now fight for the throne; whichever survives keeps our prestige intact, and our heirs will continue to rule the steppe peoples.

sentences.zh: 漢雖強，猶不能兼併匈奴，奈何亂先古之制，臣事於漢，卑辱先單于，為諸國所笑！

translations.literal: Though Han is strong, still it cannot annex the Xiongnu; how disorder the regulations of former antiquity, serving Han as subject, humiliating former Chanyus, being laughed at by the various states!
translations.idiomatic: Han may be mighty, yet it cannot swallow us whole; why overturn the ways of our fathers, bow as Han’s vassals, and shame every Chanyu who came before—only to become a laughingstock among the tribes?

sentences.zh: 雖如是而安，何以復長百蠻！

translations.literal: Though thus and secure, how again lord over the hundred barbarians!
translations.idiomatic: Even if we gained safety that way, how could we ever again command the hundred tribes?

sentences.zh: 」左伊秩訾曰：「不然。

translations.literal: The Left Yizhi said: ‘It is not so.’
translations.idiomatic: The Left Yizhi king answered: ‘That is not how it is.’

sentences.zh: 強弱有時，今漢方盛，烏孫城郭諸國皆為臣妾。

translations.literal: Strength and weakness have their times; now Han is flourishing; Wusun and the walled states all are subjects and concubines.
translations.idiomatic: Power shifts with the seasons: Han is ascendant now, and Wusun together with every oasis state already kneels as its client.

sentences.zh: 自且鞮侯單于以來，匈奴日削，不能取復，雖屈強於此，未嘗一日安也。

translations.literal: From Chanyu Qi-di-hou onward, the Xiongnu daily dwindled, unable to recover; though stubbornly strong here, never one day secure.
translations.idiomatic: Since the reign of Qi-di-hou we have shrunk every year and never won back what we lost; defiance has bought us not a single day of calm.

sentences.zh: 今事漢則安存，不事則危亡，計何以過此！

translations.literal: Now serving Han then secure survival; not serving then peril and extinction; in planning how surpass this!
translations.idiomatic: Serve Han and we survive; refuse and we perish—what strategy could be clearer?

sentences.zh: 」諸大人相難久之。

translations.literal: The great nobles mutually disputed long.
translations.idiomatic: The nobles argued back and forth for a long while.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪從其計，引眾南近塞，遣子右賢王銖婁渠堂入侍。

translations.literal: Huhanye followed his plan, led the multitude south near the passes, sent son the Right Virtuous King Zhulou Qutang to enter attendance.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye accepted the counsel, drew his people south toward the frontier, and sent his son Zhulou Qutang, the Right Virtuous king, to the Han court as a hostage.

sentences.zh: 郅支單于亦遣子右大將駒於利受入侍。

translations.literal: Chanyu Zhizhi also sent son the Right Great General Ju-yu Lishou to enter attendance.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi likewise dispatched his son Ju-yu Lishou, the Right Great general, to serve at court.

sentences.zh: 是歲，甘露元年也。

translations.literal: This year was the first year of Ganlu.
translations.idiomatic: The year was the first of the Ganlu era (53 BCE).

sentences.zh: 明年，呼韓邪單于款五原塞，願朝三年正月。

translations.literal: The next year, Chanyu Huhanye presented himself in friendship at Wuyuan Pass and requested an audience with the emperor on New Year’s Day of the third year.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Huhanye arrived peacefully at Wuyuan Pass and asked to attend the imperial New Year court in the third regnal year.

sentences.zh: 漢遣車騎都尉韓昌迎，發過所七郡郡二千騎，為陳道上。

translations.literal: Han sent Cavalry Commandant Han Chang to welcome, issuing two thousand riders from each of the seven commanderies along the route, to array the road above.
translations.idiomatic: The Han court sent Cavalry Commandant Han Chang to escort him in, levying two thousand mounted men from each of the seven jurisdictions along his path to line the highway.

sentences.zh: 單于正月朝天子於甘泉宮，漢寵際殊禮，位在諸侯王上，贊謁稱臣而不名。

translations.literal: The Chanyu in the first month courted the Son of Heaven at Ganquan Palace; Han favored him with extraordinary ritual, rank above the feudal kings; introducers announced him as subject and did not state his name.
translations.idiomatic: In the first month the Chanyu had audience with the emperor at Ganquan; Han honored him above every prince of the blood, and heralds presented him as a vassal without calling his personal name.

sentences.zh: 賜以冠帶衣裳、黃金璽戾綬、玉具劍、佩刀、弓一張、矢四發、棨戟十、安車一乘、鞍勒一縣、馬十五匹、黃金二十斤、錢二十萬、衣被七十七襲、錦繡綺縠雜帛八千匹、絮六千斤。

translations.literal: He was granted cap-belt robes and garments, a golden seal with twisted cord, a jade-mounted sword, a waist knife, one bow, four flights of arrows, ten halberds, one riding carriage, one set of saddle and bridle, fifteen horses, twenty jin of gold, two hundred thousand cash, seventy-seven suits of clothing, eight thousand bolts of brocade, figured silk, and thin silk of mixed sorts, six thousand jin of floss silk.
translations.idiomatic: The gifts included full court dress, a gold seal on twisted ribbon, a sword with jade fittings, a belt knife, a bow and four quivers of arrows, ten ceremonial halberds, a state carriage with saddle and harness, fifteen horses, twenty catties of gold, two hundred thousand strings of cash, seventy-seven changes of robes, eight thousand bolts of figured silks and gauzes, and six thousand catties of wadding silk.

sentences.zh: 禮畢，使使者道單于先行，宿長平。

translations.literal: When the rites finished, he ordered envoys to guide the Chanyu to go ahead first, lodging at Changping.
translations.idiomatic: After the ceremonies envoys escorted the Chanyu on ahead to lodge at Changping.

sentences.zh: 上自甘泉宿池陽宮。

translations.literal: The emperor himself from Ganquan lodged at Chiyang Palace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor traveled from Ganquan and spent the night at Chiyang Palace.

sentences.zh: 上登長平，詔單于毋謁，其左右當戶之群臣皆得列觀，及諸蠻夷君長王侯數萬，鹹迎於渭橋下，夾道陳。

translations.literal: The emperor ascended Changping, ordered the Chanyu not to bow; his Left and Right Household ministers and host of officials all were permitted to stand in ranks and observe, and the various barbarian chiefs, kings, and marquises numbering tens of thousands, all welcomed below Wei Bridge, arrayed along the road.
translations.idiomatic: From Changping the emperor waived the Chanyu’s obligation to kowtow while his household ministers and every barbarian chief and prince—tens of thousands in all—lined the road beneath Wei Bridge to greet him.

sentences.zh: 上登渭橋，咸稱萬歲。

translations.literal: The emperor ascended Wei Bridge; all called ‘ten thousand years.’
translations.idiomatic: When he stepped onto Wei Bridge the crowd shouted ‘Long live the Son of Heaven!’

sentences.zh: 單于就邸，留月餘，遣歸國。

translations.literal: The Chanyu went to his lodging, stayed over a month, was sent to return to his state.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu moved into his guest residence, remained more than a month, and was escorted home.

sentences.zh: 單于自請願留居光祿塞下，有急保漢受降城。

translations.literal: The Chanyu himself requested wishing to remain dwelling below Guanglu Pass; if urgent, protect Han’s Surrender-Accepting City.
translations.idiomatic: He asked permission to camp below Guanglu Pass so that, should trouble arise, he could shelter behind Han’s Surrender-Accepting City.

sentences.zh: 漢遣長樂衛尉高昌侯董忠、車騎都尉韓昌將騎萬六千，又發邊郡士馬以千數，送單于出朔方雞鹿塞。

translations.literal: Han sent Commandant of the Guards of Changle and Marquis of Gaocheng Dong Zhong and Cavalry Commandant Han Chang to lead sixteen thousand riders, also levied frontier commanderies’ soldiers and horses numbering in the thousands, and escorted the Chanyu out through Jilu Pass in Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: Han detached sixteen thousand horsemen under Weiquan of Changle Dong Zhong, marquis of Gaocheng, and Cavalry Commandant Han Chang, reinforced by thousands more levied from the border commands, and convoyed the Chanyu through Jilu Pass in Shuofang.

sentences.zh: 詔忠等留衛單于，助誅不服，又轉邊谷米E05F，前後三萬四千斛，給贍其食。

translations.literal: An edict ordered Zhong and others to remain guarding the Chanyu, assisting to execute those not obedient; also forwarded grain from the border commands; before and after thirty-four thousand hu, to supply their food.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor told Dong Zhong’s detachment to stay and protect the Chanyu, help suppress dissenters, and forwarded thirty-four thousand hu of frontier grain and provisions in successive shipments to feed his people.

sentences.zh: 是歲，郅支單於亦遣使奉獻，漢遇之甚厚。

translations.literal: This year, Chanyu Zhizhi also sent envoys presenting tribute; Han treated them very generously.
translations.idiomatic: That same year Zhizhi sent tribute envoys as well, and Han received them with lavish courtesy.

sentences.zh: 明年，兩單于俱遣使朝獻，漢待呼韓邪使有加。

translations.literal: The next year, both Chanyus together sent envoys to court and present tribute; Han treated Huhanye’s envoys with additions.
translations.idiomatic: The following year both rival Chanyus sent tribute missions, but Han showed marked preference to Huhanye’s embassy.

sentences.zh: 明年，呼韓邪單于復入朝，禮賜如初，加衣百一十襲，錦帛九千匹，絮八千斤。

translations.literal: The next year, Chanyu Huhanye again entered court; ritual grants as at first, adding one hundred ten suits of clothing, nine thousand bolts of brocade silk, eight thousand jin of floss silk.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye came to court again the next year and received the same honors as before, plus another hundred and ten changes of robe, nine thousand bolts of figured silk, and eight thousand catties of wadding.

sentences.zh: 以有屯兵，故不復發騎為送。

translations.literal: Because there were stationed troops, therefore did not again issue riders to escort.
translations.idiomatic: Because Han troops were already encamped with him, no cavalry escort was sent this time.

sentences.zh: 始，郅支單于以為呼韓邪降漢，兵弱不能復自還，即引其眾西，欲攻定右地。

translations.literal: At first, Chanyu Zhizhi thought Huhanye had surrendered to Han, troops weak unable again to return himself, then led his multitude west, wishing to attack and settle the right territory.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi assumed that Huhanye’s submission to Han had ruined his army and he could never march home; he therefore dragged his people westward intending to conquer and hold the western steppe.

sentences.zh: 又屠耆單于小弟本侍呼韓邪，亦亡之右地，收兩兄余兵得數千人，自立為伊利目單於，道逢郅支，合戰，郅支殺之，並其兵五萬餘人。

translations.literal: Also Tuqi’s younger brother originally attending Huhanye, also fled to the right territory, gathered two elder brothers’ remnant troops obtaining several thousand men, established himself as Chanyu Yilimu; on the road encountering Zhizhi, joined battle; Zhizhi killed him, annexed his troops more than fifty thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Tuqi’s youngest brother, who had been in Huhanye’s train, also fled west, scraped together his brothers’ remnants—several thousand riders—and styled himself Chanyu Yilimu. He ran into Zhizhi on the march; Zhizhi slew him and absorbed more than fifty thousand of his warriors.

sentences.zh: 聞漢出兵、谷助呼韓邪，即遂留居右地。

translations.literal: Hearing Han issued troops and grain assisting Huhanye, then thus remained dwelling in the right territory.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that Han was feeding and reinforcing Huhanye, he stayed put in the western pastures.

sentences.zh: 自度力不能定匈奴，乃益西近烏孫，欲與並力，遣使見小昆彌烏就屠。

translations.literal: Self estimating strength unable to stabilize the Xiongnu, then increasingly west approached Wusun, wishing to combine strength, sent envoys to see Lesser Kunmi Wujiutu.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing he could not unify the Xiongnu alone, he edged farther west toward Wusun hoping for an alliance and sent envoys to the junior kunmi Wujiutu.

sentences.zh: 烏就屠見呼韓邪為漢所擁，郅支亡虜，欲攻之以稱漢，乃殺郅支使，持頭送都護在所，發八千騎迎郅支。

translations.literal: Wujiutu seeing Huhanye was embraced by Han, Zhizhi a fleeing captive, wished to attack him to proclaim loyalty to Han, then killed Zhizhi’s envoy, carried the head sent to where the Protector-General was, issued eight thousand riders to welcome Zhizhi.
translations.idiomatic: Wujiutu noticed that Han favored Huhanye while Zhizhi was a beaten fugitive; eager to prove his loyalty to Han he murdered Zhizhi’s envoy, sent the head to the Protector-General’s headquarters, and ostensibly mobilized eight thousand riders to “meet” Zhizhi.

sentences.zh: 郅支見烏孫兵多，其使又不反，勒兵逢擊烏孫，破之。

translations.literal: Zhizhi seeing Wusun troops many, his envoy again not returning, marshaled troops encountering and striking Wusun, defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi saw Wusun’s numbers and that his own envoy never returned; he brought his army to bear and smashed them.

sentences.zh: 因北擊烏揭，烏揭降。

translations.literal: Thereupon north struck Wujie; Wujie surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: He then swung north against Wujie and forced its submission.

sentences.zh: 發其兵西破堅昆，北降丁令，並三國。

translations.literal: Issued their troops west defeated Jiankun, north subdued Dingling, annexed three states.
translations.idiomatic: With those troops he crushed Jiankun in the west, brought the Dingling to heel in the north, and folded three peoples into one realm.

sentences.zh: 數遣兵擊烏孫，常勝之。

translations.literal: Repeatedly sent troops to strike Wusun, constantly defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: He raided Wusun again and again and usually carried the field.

sentences.zh: 堅昆東去單于庭七千里，南去車師五千里，郅支留都之。

translations.literal: Jiankun lies seven thousand li east of the Chanyu court and five thousand li south of Cheshi; Zhizhi stayed on and made it his capital.
translations.idiomatic: Jiankun stood seven thousand li east of the old Chanyu headquarters and five thousand li south of Cheshi; Zhizhi made it his seat.

sentences.zh: 元帝初即位，呼韓邪單于復上書，言民眾困乏。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan first ascended, Chanyu Huhanye again submitted a memorial, speaking that the people masses were destitute.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Yuan’s reign Huhanye memorialized again that his people were starving and exhausted.

sentences.zh: 漢詔雲中、五原郡轉谷二萬斛以給焉。

translations.literal: Han ordered Yunzhong and Wuyuan commanderies to transfer grain twenty thousand hu to supply them.
translations.idiomatic: The court ordered Yunzhong and Wuyuan to ship twenty thousand hu of grain to relieve him.

sentences.zh: 郅支單于自以道遠，又怨漢擁護呼韓邪，遣使上書求侍子。

translations.literal: Chanyu Zhizhi himself considering the road distant, again resenting Han’s cherishing Huhanye, sent envoys submitting memorial requesting the hostage son.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi felt himself too remote and bitter that Han coddled Huhanye; he sent envoys demanding the return of his hostage son.

sentences.zh: 漢遣谷吉送之，郅支殺吉。

translations.literal: Han sent Gu Ji to escort him; Zhizhi killed Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Han dispatched Gu Ji to escort the prince home; Zhizhi murdered him.

sentences.zh: 漢不知吉音問，而匈奴降者言聞甌脫皆殺之。

translations.literal: Han received no word from Gu Ji, but Xiongnu surrenderers reported hearing that those at the frontier watch-posts had killed them all.
translations.idiomatic: Court officials heard nothing from Gu Ji, yet defectors claimed that frontier sentries along his route had slaughtered the entire mission.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪單于使來，漢輒簿責之甚急。

translations.literal: Whenever Huhanye’s envoys arrived, Han repeatedly interrogated them harshly on the record.
translations.idiomatic: Each time Huhanye sent envoys the Han court subjected them to blistering official interrogations.

sentences.zh: 明年，漢遣車騎都尉韓昌、光祿大夫張猛送呼韓邪單于侍子，求問吉等，因赦其罪，勿令自疑。

translations.literal: The next year, Han sent Cavalry Commandant Han Chang and Grandee of Brilliant Virtue Zhang Meng to escort Chanyu Huhanye’s hostage son, seek news of Gu Ji and his party, thereby pardon their crimes, not letting them harbor suspicion.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Han Chang and Zhang Meng, Grandee of Brilliant Virtue, convoyed Huhanye’s heir home while pressing for news of Gu Ji and offering amnesty so the Chanyu would not suspect Han of ill intent.

sentences.zh: 昌、猛見單于民眾益盛，塞下禽獸盡，單于足以自衛，不畏郅支。

translations.literal: Chang and Meng seeing the Chanyu’s people increasingly flourishing, below the passes beasts and birds exhausted, the Chanyu sufficient for self-defense, not fearing Zhizhi.
translations.idiomatic: Chang and Meng saw Huhanye’s following swell until game vanished along the frontier; they judged him strong enough to stand off Zhizhi without leaning on Han.

sentences.zh: 聞其大臣多勸單于北歸者，恐北去後難約束，昌、猛即與為盟約曰：「自今以來，漢與匈奴合為一家，世世毋得相詐相攻。

translations.literal: Hearing his great ministers many urging the Chanyu north to return, fearing after north departing hard to restrain, Chang and Meng then with him made covenant saying: ‘From now onward, Han and the Xiongnu combine as one family; generation after generation must not mutually deceive mutually attack.’
translations.idiomatic: Learning that many nobles wanted to march north—where Han could not control them—Chang and Meng swore an oath with the Chanyu: ‘Henceforth Han and the Xiongnu are one house; neither side may betray or strike the other for any generation.’

sentences.zh: 有竊盜者，相報，行其誅，償其物；

translations.literal: There being thieves, mutually report, carry out their execution, compensate their goods;
translations.idiomatic: If bandits appear, each side shall inform the other, punish the culprits, and make restitution;

sentences.zh: 有寇，發兵相助。

translations.literal: There being raiders, issue troops mutually assist.
translations.idiomatic: if raiders threaten either realm, both shall send troops to help.

sentences.zh: 漢與匈奴敢先背約者，受天不祥。

translations.literal: Han and the Xiongnu whoever dares first betray the covenant, receive Heaven’s ill-omen.
translations.idiomatic: Whichever side breaks this pact first shall suffer Heaven’s curse.

sentences.zh: 令其世世子孫盡如盟。

translations.literal: Order their generation after generation descendants all like the covenant.
translations.idiomatic: Let every descendant abide by these terms forever.

sentences.zh: 」昌、猛與單于及大臣俱登匈奴諾水東山，刑白馬，單于以徑路刀金留犁撓酒，以老上單於所破月氏王頭為飲器者共飲血盟。

translations.literal: Chang and Meng together with the Chanyu and great ministers all ascended the Xiongnu Nu River eastern hill, executed white horse; the Chanyu with Jinglu knife and Jinliuli stirring wine, with Old Shang Chanyu’s captured Yuezhi king head made drinking vessel, together drank blood covenant.
translations.idiomatic: Chang, Meng, the Chanyu, and his nobles climbed the hill east of the Nu River, sacrificed a white horse, mixed wine with the sacred knife and ladle, and sealed the pact with blood drunk from the skull cup fashioned from the Yuezhi king whom Old Shang Chanyu had defeated.

sentences.zh: 昌、猛還奏事，公卿議者以為：「單于保塞為籓，雖欲北去，猶不能為危害。

translations.literal: Chang and Meng returned and reported; deliberating ministers argued: ‘The Chanyu guards the passes as our bulwark; even if he wishes to withdraw northward he still cannot inflict real harm.’
translations.idiomatic: Back at court the high ministers argued: ‘The Chanyu shields our frontier; even if he wanders north he cannot seriously threaten us.’

sentences.zh: 昌、猛擅以漢國世世子孫與夷狄詛盟，令單于得以惡言上告於天，羞國家，傷威重，不可得行。

translations.literal: Yet Chang and Meng on their own authority bound Han’s heirs for all generations to barbarians by blood oath, letting the Chanyu reproach Han with imprecations before Heaven—disgracing the dynasty and damaging its majesty—such a deed cannot be permitted.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Chang and Meng had shackled every future emperor to barbarians by blood oath and handed the Chanyu a stick to beat Han with before Heaven—a disgrace that erodes imperial prestige and cannot be allowed.’

sentences.zh: 宜遣使往告祠天，與解盟。

translations.literal: It is fitting to send envoys to report to Heaven at sacrifice and annul the covenant.
translations.idiomatic: They urged dispatching envoys to notify Heaven at the suburban altar and annul the treaty.’

sentences.zh: 昌、猛奉使無狀，罪至不道。

translations.literal: Chang and Meng disgraced their mission; their offense warrants the capital crime of ‘lack of principle.’
translations.idiomatic: Chang and Meng had abused their commissions and deserved execution for gross misconduct.’

sentences.zh: 」上薄其過，有詔昌、猛以贖論，勿解盟。

translations.literal: The emperor slighted their faults, issued edict Chang and Meng by ransom adjudication, do not dissolve covenant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor minimized their offense, fined them in lieu of death, and left the covenant intact.

sentences.zh: 其後呼韓邪竟北歸庭，人眾稍稍歸之，國中遂定。

translations.literal: Afterward Huhanye ultimately north returned court; people multitudes gradually returned to him; within the state then stabilized.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye eventually rode north to refound his court; tribesmen drifted back until the realm settled.

sentences.zh: 郅支既殺使者，自知負漢，又聞呼韓邪益強，恐見襲擊，欲遠去。

translations.literal: Zhizhi having killed the envoy, himself knew he betrayed Han, again hearing Huhanye increasingly strong, feared seeing attack and strike, wished to go far away.
translations.idiomatic: After murdering Han’s envoy Zhizhi knew he had wronged the empire; reports that Huhanye was growing stronger convinced him he would be struck, so he resolved to flee deep into the west.

sentences.zh: 會康居王數為烏孫所困，與諸翕侯計，以為匈奴大國，烏孫素服屬之，今郅支單于困厄在外，可迎置東邊，使合兵取烏孫以立之，長無匈奴憂矣。

translations.literal: It happened the King of Kangju repeatedly was distressed by Wusun; with various Xi-hou he planned, thinking the Xiongnu a great state, Wusun plain submitted to them; now Chanyu Zhizhi distressed outside, can welcome and place east border, order combine troops take Wusun to establish him, long without Xiongnu worry.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Kangju, worn down by Wusun raids, took counsel with his nobles: the Xiongnu were still a great power and Wusun had long obeyed them; Chanyu Zhizhi, though exiled, could be welcomed to Kangju’s eastern marches so their armies might conquer Wusun and enthrone him—after which Kangju need never fear the Xiongnu again.

sentences.zh: 即使使至堅昆通語郅支。

translations.literal: Immediately sent envoys to Jiankun to communicate speech with Zhizhi.
translations.idiomatic: They promptly sent envoys through Jiankun to open talks with Zhizhi.

sentences.zh: 郅支素恐，又怨烏孫，聞康居計，大說，遂與相結，引兵而西。

translations.literal: Zhizhi plain feared, again resented Wusun, hearing Kangju’s plan, greatly pleased, then with them knotted alliance, led troops and west.
translations.idiomatic: Zhizhi was already anxious and nursed a grudge against Wusun; Kangju’s proposal delighted him, he accepted the alliance, and marched west.

sentences.zh: 康居亦遣貴人，橐它驢馬數千匹，迎郅支。

translations.literal: Kangju also sent nobles, camels and donkeys and horses several thousand head, welcoming Zhizhi.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju dispatched nobles with thousands of camels, horses, and pack animals to meet him.

sentences.zh: 郅支人眾中寒道死，余財三千人到康居。

translations.literal: Zhizhi’s people multitude amid cold on the road died; remainder only three thousand men reached Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: Cold and hardship winnowed his column on the march; barely three thousand survivors entered Kangju.

sentences.zh: 其後，都護甘延壽與副陳湯發兵即康居誅斬郅支，語在《延壽、湯傳》。

translations.literal: Afterward, Protector-General Gan Yanshou with deputy Chen Tang issued troops arriving Kangju executed and beheaded Zhizhi; the words are in the ‘Yanshou and Tang Traditions.’
translations.idiomatic: Later Protector-General Gan Yanshou and his deputy Chen Tang marched into Kangju and executed Zhizhi; the account is given in their biographies.

sentences.zh: 郅支既誅，呼韓邪單于且喜且懼，上書言曰：「常願謁見天子，誠以郅支在西方，恐其與烏孫俱來擊臣，以故未得至漢。

translations.literal: Zhizhi having been executed, Chanyu Huhanye both rejoiced and feared, submitting memorial speaking: ‘Constantly wished to pay court seeing Son of Heaven; truly because Zhizhi was in the west, feared he together with Wusun both come strike your servant; by reason not able to reach Han.’
translations.idiomatic: With Zhizhi dead Huhanye felt both relief and dread; he memorialized: ‘I have long wished to attend Your Majesty, yet while Zhizhi held the west I feared he might join Wusun in attacking me—that is why I could not come to Han.’

sentences.zh: 今郅支已伏誅，願入朝見。

translations.literal: Now Zhizhi already submitted execution; wish to enter court and see.
translations.idiomatic: Now that Zhizhi has paid with his life I beg leave to attend court.’

sentences.zh: 」竟寧元年，單于復入朝，禮賜如初，加衣服錦帛絮，皆倍於黃龍時。

translations.literal: The first year of Jingning, the Chanyu again entered court; ritual grants as at first, adding clothing brocade silk floss, all doubled compared to Huanglong times.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Jingning (33 BCE) he returned to court with the same ceremonial bounty as before, but silks, robes, and wadding were twice what he had received in the Huanglong era.

sentences.zh: 單于自言願婿漢氏以自親。

translations.literal: The Chanyu himself spoke wishing to marry Han clan thereby himself draw close.
translations.idiomatic: He declared his desire to marry into the imperial house and bind the two realms by kinship.

sentences.zh: 元帝以後宮良家子王牆字昭君賜單于。

translations.literal: Emperor Yuan took from the rear palace a woman of good family Wang Qiang, courtesy name Zhaojun, and bestowed her on the Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan gave the Chanyu a palace lady of respectable birth named Wang Qiang, whose courtesy name was Zhaojun.

sentences.zh: 單于歡喜，上書願保塞上谷以西至敦煌，傳之無窮，請罷邊備塞吏卒，以休天子人民。

translations.literal: The Chanyu rejoiced, submitted memorial wishing to guard the passes from Shanggu west to Dunhuang, transmit it without end, requesting dismiss border preparations pass officials and soldiers, thereby to rest the Son of Heaven’s people.
translations.idiomatic: Delighted, the Chanyu memorialized his pledge to hold the frontier from Shanggu west to Dunhuang for all generations and asked Han to withdraw its garrison troops so both ruler and subjects might enjoy peace.

sentences.zh: 天子令下有司議，議者皆以為便。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven ordered subordinate offices deliberate; deliberators all deemed it convenient.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor referred the matter to his officials, who unanimously pronounced it expedient.

sentences.zh: 郎中侯應習邊事，以為不可許。

translations.literal: Gentleman Hou Ying was versed in border affairs; he deemed it must not be permitted.
translations.idiomatic: Palace Gentleman Hou Ying, who knew the frontier well, argued that the request must be refused.

sentences.zh: 上問狀，應曰：

translations.literal: The emperor asked the circumstances; Ying said:
translations.idiomatic: The emperor demanded particulars; Hou Ying replied:

sentences.zh: 周、秦以來，匈奴暴桀，寇侵邊境，漢興，尤被其害。

translations.literal: From Zhou and Qin onward, the Xiongnu violently fierce, raided and invaded the borderlands; Han arose, especially suffered their harm.
translations.idiomatic: Since Zhou and Qin times the Xiongnu have raided our borders with relentless ferocity, and the Han realm has borne the brunt of their attacks.

sentences.zh: 臣聞北邊塞至遼東，外有陰山，東西千餘里，草木茂盛，多禽獸，本冒頓單于依阻其中，治作弓矢，來出為寇，是其苑囿也。

translations.literal: Your servant hears from the northern border passes to Liaodong, outside there is Yin Mountain, east-west more than a thousand li, plants and trees lush, many birds and beasts; originally Chanyu Modun relied and obstructed therein, manufactured bows and arrows, came out as raiders—it was their hunting park.
translations.idiomatic: North of the frontier, from the passes to Liaodong, runs Yin Mountain—more than a thousand li of meadow where Modun once sheltered, forged bows and arrows, and sallied forth to plunder; it was his royal hunting ground.

sentences.zh: 至孝武世，出師征伐，斥奪此地，攘之於幕北。

translations.literal: Reaching Emperor Xiaowu’s age, issued armies campaigning, expelled and seized this land, drove them to north of the desert.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu sent armies that wrested that ground away and pushed the Xiongnu north of the Gobi.

sentences.zh: 建塞徼，起亭隧，築外城，設屯戍以守之，然後邊境得用少安。

translations.literal: Built border barriers, raised beacon towers, constructed outer walls, established garrison guards to defend them; afterward the borderlands obtained thereby slight peace.
translations.idiomatic: We ringed it with barriers, signal towers, outer walls, and rotating garrisons—only then did the frontier know a measure of calm.

sentences.zh: 幕北地乎，少草木，多大沙，匈奴來寇，少所蔽隱，從塞以南，逕深山谷，往來差難。

translations.literal: North of the desert the land—few plants and trees, much great sand; when Xiongnu come raiding there is little cover; south of the wall lie deep ravines and narrow valleys, so passage back and forth is difficult.
translations.idiomatic: North of the desert lies barren sand where raiders find little cover, while south of the wall deep ravines make any march arduous.

sentences.zh: 邊長老言匈奴失陰山之後，過之未嘗不哭也。

translations.literal: Border elders speak that after the Xiongnu lost Yin Mountain, passing it never did not weep.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier elders say the Xiongnu still weep whenever they ride past lost Yin Mountain.

sentences.zh: 如罷備塞戍卒，示夷狄之大利，不可一也。

translations.literal: If dismiss preparation pass garrison soldiers, show barbarians great profit—cannot, first reason.
translations.idiomatic: First: dismantling the garrisons would hand the barbarians a prize they must not be offered.

sentences.zh: 今聖德廣被，天覆匈奴，匈奴得蒙全活之恩，稽首來臣。

translations.literal: Now sagely virtue broadly covers; Heaven overspreads the Xiongnu; the Xiongnu obtain to receive whole-alive grace, kowtow head come subject.
translations.idiomatic: Today Your Majesty’s grace blankets Heaven itself; the Xiongnu owe their survival to you and have bowed as vassals.

sentences.zh: 夫夷狄之情，困則卑順，強則驕逆，天性然也。

translations.literal: As for barbarians’ disposition, distressed then humble obedient, strong then arrogant rebellious—Heaven-nature thus.
translations.idiomatic: Steppe peoples humble themselves when weak and turn arrogant when strong—it is their nature.

sentences.zh: 前以罷外城，省亭隧，今裁足以候望通烽火而已。

translations.literal: Formerly by dismissing outer walls, reducing beacon towers, now only sufficient for lookout connect beacon fires alone.
translations.idiomatic: We already dismantled outer walls and thinned the beacon chain until today it barely suffices for watch and signal.

sentences.zh: 古者安不忘危，不可復罷，二也。

translations.literal: Anciently in peace not forgetting peril; cannot again dismiss—second reason.
translations.idiomatic: Second: the sages stayed vigilant even in peace—we cannot strip the wall further.

sentences.zh: 中國有禮義之教、刑罰之誅，愚民猶尚犯禁，又況單于，能必其眾不犯約哉！

translations.literal: Central States have ritual-duty teaching and punishments’ executions; foolish people still moreover violate prohibitions—how much more the Chanyu, able surely his multitude not violate covenant!
translations.idiomatic: Even under ritual and law our own dull peasants break edicts—how can we be sure a Chanyu will bind every tribesman to the treaty?

sentences.zh: 三也。

translations.literal: Third reason.
translations.idiomatic: Third.

sentences.zh: 自中國尚建關梁以制諸侯，所以絕臣下之凱欲也。

translations.literal: Since Central States still built passes and bridges to control feudal lords—thereby to cut off ministers’ covetous desires.
translations.idiomatic: China itself maintains barrier gates to curb its nobles and choke off treasonous ambition.

sentences.zh: 設塞徼，置屯戍，非獨為匈奴而已，亦為諸屬國降民，本故匈奴之人，恐其思舊逃亡，四也。

translations.literal: Establish border barriers, place garrison guards—not solely for the Xiongnu alone; also for various dependent-state surrendered people, originally former Xiongnu persons, fearing they think of old and flee—fourth reason.
translations.idiomatic: Fourth: the line also pens surrendered Xiongnu dwelling in dependent states who might otherwise flee homeward on a whim.

sentences.zh: 近西羌保塞，與漢人交通，吏民貪利，侵盜其畜產、妻子，以此怨恨，起而背畔，世世不絕。

translations.literal: Recently western Qiang guard passes, traffic with Han people; officials and people greedy profit, encroach steal their livestock and goods, wives and children; by this resent hatred, rise and rebel—generation after generation unending.
translations.idiomatic: Western Qiang living along the wall trade with Han rustlers who steal their herds and kin; bitterness sparks rebellion generation after generation.

sentences.zh: 今罷乘塞，則生嫚易分爭之漸，五也。

translations.literal: Now dismiss riding the passes, then gives rise to insult-easy divide quarrel beginnings—fifth reason.
translations.idiomatic: Fifth: pulling pickets off the wall invites contempt and petty fights that spiral into war.

sentences.zh: 往者從軍多沒不還者，子孫貧困，一旦亡出，從其親威，六也。

translations.literal: Formerly following army many sunk not returned; descendants poor distressed; once flee out, follow their relatives—sixth reason.
translations.idiomatic: Sixth: countless soldiers died on campaign; their impoverished heirs slip across the line to join kin among the nomads.

sentences.zh: 又邊人奴婢愁苦，欲亡者多，曰「聞匈奴中樂，無奈候望急何！

translations.literal: Moreover border people’s slaves and maids suffer bitterly; many wish to flee, saying, ‘We hear life among the Xiongnu is pleasant—yet what can we do about these relentless patrols!’
translations.idiomatic: Frontier slaves groan under harsh duty and mutter that nomad tents sound idyllic while Han watchposts never ease.

sentences.zh: 」然時有亡出塞者，七也。

translations.literal: Yet at times there are those who flee out past the passes—seventh reason.
translations.idiomatic: And some still desert across the wall—seventh.

sentences.zh: 盜賊桀黠，群輩犯法，如其窘急，亡走北出，則不可制，八也。

translations.literal: Robbers and bandits fierce cunning, bands violate law; if they distressed urgent, flee north out—then cannot control—eighth reason.
translations.idiomatic: Eighth: gangs of outlaws, when cornered, bolt north beyond reach of Han law.

sentences.zh: 起塞以來百有餘年，非皆以土垣也，或因山岩石，木柴僵落，溪谷水門，稍稍平之，卒徒築治，功費久遠，不可勝計。

translations.literal: Since raising the frontier defenses more than a hundred years ago—not everything has been earthen ramparts; some sections follow mountain rock, stake palisades across streams, or dam ravines—crews have graded every yard and maintained them at enormous lasting cost beyond reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: For over a century we have fused rammed earth with cliffs, timber palisades, and river gates—works whose cost in labor and treasure cannot be reckoned.

sentences.zh: 臣恐議者不深慮其終始，欲以一切省徭戍，十年之外，百歲之內，卒有它變，障塞破壞，亭隧滅絕，當更發屯繕治，累世之功不可卒復，九也。

translations.literal: Your servant fears deliberators do not deeply consider its beginning and end, wishing by wholesale cut corvée garrison; beyond ten years, within a hundred years, suddenly there is other change—barriers broken, beacons extinguished—must again issue garrison repair; piled generations’ achievement cannot suddenly restore—ninth reason.
translations.idiomatic: Ninth: advisers seek quick savings in labor, yet within decades some crisis could smash what generations built beyond hope of swift repair.

sentences.zh: 如罷戍卒、省候望，單于自以保塞守禦，必深德漢，請求無已。

translations.literal: If we strip garrisons and shrinks patrols while the Chanyu imagines he alone shields the passes, he will feel endlessly indebted to Han and his demands will never cease.
translations.idiomatic: Withdraw the watch and the Chanyu will fancy himself sole guardian of the wall, feel endlessly entitled to Han favors, and demand without cease.

sentences.zh: 小失其意，則不可測。

translations.literal: Slightly lose his intent—then cannot be fathomed.
translations.idiomatic: The slightest slight could provoke unknowable wrath.

sentences.zh: 開夷狄之隙，虧中國之固，十也。

translations.literal: Open barbarians’ crack, diminish Central States’ solidity—tenth reason.
translations.idiomatic: Tenth: it would invite barbarian intrigue and sap China’s defenses.

sentences.zh: 非所以永持至安，威制百蠻之長策也。

translations.literal: Not the means eternally to hold utmost peace, awe-control hundred barbarians’ long strategy.
translations.idiomatic: This is no way to secure lasting peace or keep the hundred tribes in awe.

sentences.zh: 對奏，天子有詔：「勿議罷邊塞事。

translations.literal: The reply was presented; the Son of Heaven had an edict: ‘Do not deliberate dismissing border pass affairs.’
translations.idiomatic: The emperor decreed: ‘Take no further counsel on abandoning the frontier wall.’

sentences.zh: 」使車騎將軍口諭單于曰：「單于上書願罷北邊吏士屯戍，子孫世世保塞。

translations.literal: He ordered the General of Chariots and Cavalry orally instruct the Chanyu saying: ‘The Chanyu submitted memorial wishing to dismiss northern border officials and soldiers garrison guard; descendants generation after generation guard the passes.’
translations.idiomatic: The General of Chariots and Cavalry was told to explain orally: ‘Your memorial asks Han to pull its northern garrisons while your heirs forever shield the frontier.’

sentences.zh: 單于鄉慕禮義，所以為民計者甚厚，此長久之策也，朕甚嘉之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu inclines toward admires ritual duty; what thereby plans for the people is very generous—this is a long-lasting strategy; We greatly praise it.
translations.idiomatic: Your reverence for civilization and care for your people mark a policy built for ages; I commend it warmly.’

sentences.zh: 中國四方皆有關梁障塞，非獨以備塞外也，亦以防中國奸邪放縱，出為寇害，故明法度以專眾心也。

translations.literal: Central States four quarters all have passes bridges barriers—not solely to prepare against outside passes; also to guard against Central States wickedness unrestrained, going out as raiders harm; therefore clarify laws and regulations to unify multitude hearts.
translations.idiomatic: China rings itself with gates and ramparts not only against outsiders but to cage our own criminals who would flee beyond the law—barriers focus every subject on duty.’

sentences.zh: 敬諭單于之意，朕無疑焉。

translations.literal: Respectfully informing the Chanyu’s intent—We have no doubt therein.
translations.idiomatic: I trust your goodwill without reservation.’

sentences.zh: 為單于怪其不罷，故使大司馬車騎將軍嘉曉單于。

translations.literal: Because the Chanyu wondered at its not being dismissed, therefore sent Grand Marshal General of Chariots and Cavalry Jia to enlighten the Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Lest you wonder why the walls stay manned, Grand Marshal Wang Jia, General of Chariots and Cavalry, clarifies the matter.’

sentences.zh: 」單于謝曰：「愚不知大計，天子幸使大臣告語，甚厚！」

translations.literal: The Chanyu apologized saying: ‘Stupid, did not know great plan; Son of Heaven fortunately ordered great ministers to announce—very generous!’
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu bowed: ‘I was blind to the larger design; Your Majesty’s kindness in sending ministers to instruct me is overwhelming.’

sentences.zh: 初，左伊秩訾為呼韓邪畫計歸漢，竟以安定。

translations.literal: At first, the Left Yizhi planned for Huhanye’s scheme to return to Han; ultimately thereby stabilized.
translations.idiomatic: Long ago the Left Yizhi king had urged Huhanye toward Han, and that counsel brought peace.

sentences.zh: 其後或讒伊秩訾自伐其功，常鞅鞅，呼韓邪疑之。

translations.literal: Afterward some slandered Yizhi as boasting his own achievement; constantly discontented; Huhanye suspected him.
translations.idiomatic: Later rivals accused the Yizhi king of crowing over his success; he grew sullen and Huhanye began to doubt him.

sentences.zh: 左伊秩訾懼誅，將其眾千餘人降漢，漢以為關內侯，食邑三百戶，令佩其王印綬。

translations.literal: The Left Yizhi feared execution, led his multitude over a thousand to surrender to Han; Han made him Marquis Within the Passes, fief three hundred households, ordered him wear his king seal-ribbon.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing execution he brought more than a thousand followers to Han, which ennobled him as marquis within the passes with three hundred taxable households and let him keep his royal seal and sash.

sentences.zh: 及竟寧中，呼韓邪來朝，與伊穆訾相見，謝曰：「王為我計甚厚，令匈奴至今安寧，王之力也，德豈可忘！

translations.literal: When in Jingning, Huhanye came to court, met Yimuze, apologized saying: ‘King for me planned very generously, ordering the Xiongnu until now peaceful—it is the king’s force; grace how can be forgotten!’
translations.idiomatic: During the Jingning era Huhanye attended court and met the Yizhi king. ‘Your plans for me were generous,’ he said; ‘the quiet we enjoy is your doing—how could I forget your kindness?’

sentences.zh: 我失王意，使王去不復顧留，皆我過也。

translations.literal: I lost the king’s intent, causing the king to depart not again care to stay—all my faults.
translations.idiomatic: I misread you and let you leave without pressing you to stay—that fault is mine alone.’

sentences.zh: 今欲白天子，請王歸庭。

translations.literal: Now wish to report to Son of Heaven, request king return court.
translations.idiomatic: I mean to ask the Son of Heaven to send you home to my court.’

sentences.zh: 」伊秩訾曰：「單于賴天命，自歸於漢，得以安寧，單于神靈，天子之晁也，我安得力！

translations.literal: Yizhi said: ‘The Chanyu relies on Heaven’s mandate, himself returned to Han, obtained thereby peace—the Chanyu’s spiritual power is the Son of Heaven’s canopy; how could I supply strength!’
translations.idiomatic: The Yizhi king replied: ‘Your fortune rests on Heaven and on bowing to Han—the emperor’s favor is your canopy; I lent no strength of my own!’

sentences.zh: 既已降漢，又復歸匈奴，是兩心也。

translations.literal: Having already surrendered to Han, again returning to the Xiongnu—this is two hearts.
translations.idiomatic: Once I submitted to Han, returning north would brand me a double-dealer.’

sentences.zh: 願為單于侍使於漢，不敢聽命。

translations.literal: Wish to be for the Chanyu attendant envoy at Han; dare not heed command.
translations.idiomatic: Let me remain your envoy at the Han court; I cannot obey your summons home.’

sentences.zh: 」單于固請不能得而歸。

translations.literal: The Chanyu firmly requested but could not obtain and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye pressed again and again, failed to win him over, and departed.

sentences.zh: 王昭君號寧胡閼氏，生一男伊屠智牙師，為右日逐王。

translations.literal: Wang Zhaojun titled Pacify-the-Barbarians Chanyu Consort; bore one son Yitu Zhiyashi, became Right Rizhu King.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Wang Zhaojun received the title Consort Who Pacifies the Hu and bore a son, Yitu Zhiyashi, whom Huhanye named Right Rizhu king.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪立二十八年，建始二年死。

translations.literal: Huhanye established twenty-eight years; died second year of Jianshi.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye reigned twenty-eight years and died in the second year of Jianshi (31 BCE).

sentences.zh: 始，呼韓邪嬖左伊秩訾兄呼衍王女二人。

translations.literal: At first, Huhanye favored the Left Yizhi’s elder brother Huyan Wang’s two daughters.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye had long favored two daughters of the Huyan king, elder brother of the Left Yizhi king.

sentences.zh: 長女顓渠閼氏，生二子，長曰且莫車，次曰囊知牙斯。

translations.literal: Elder daughter Zhuqu Chanyu Consort bore two sons; eldest called Qie Moche, next called Nangzhiyasi.
translations.idiomatic: The elder became Principal Consort Zhuqu and bore two sons: Qie Moche the eldest and Nangzhiyasi the younger.

sentences.zh: 少女為大閼氏，生四子，長曰雕陶莫皋，次曰且糜胥，皆長於且莫車，少子鹹、樂二人，皆小子囊知牙斯。

translations.literal: Younger daughter became Great Chanyu Consort, bore four sons; eldest called Diaotaomo Gao, next called Qie Mixu—both older than Qie Moche; younger sons Xian and Le two persons—all younger than Nangzhiyasi.
translations.idiomatic: The younger daughter became senior consort and bore four sons: Diaotaomo Gao and Qie Mixu, both older than Qie Moche, plus two youngest boys named Xian and Le, each junior to Nangzhiyasi.

sentences.zh: 又它閼氏子十餘人。

translations.literal: Also other Chanyu Consorts’ sons more than ten persons.
translations.idiomatic: His other consorts gave him more than ten additional sons.

sentences.zh: 顓渠閼氏貴，且莫車愛。

translations.literal: Zhuqu Chanyu Consort noble; Qie Moche beloved.
translations.idiomatic: Principal Consort Zhuqu stood highest in rank and Qie Moche was his favorite.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪病且死，欲立且莫車，其母顓渠閼氏曰：「匈奴亂十餘年，不絕如發，賴蒙漢力，故得復安。

translations.literal: Huhanye sick and dying, wished to establish Qie Moche; his mother Zhuqu Chanyu Consort said: ‘The Xiongnu chaotic more than ten years, unbroken like hair-thread; relied received Han strength; therefore obtained again peace.’
translations.idiomatic: As Huhanye lay dying he meant to name Qie Moche heir, but Zhuqu warned: ‘For ten years our realm hung by a thread until Han restored us—’

sentences.zh: 今平定未久，人民創艾戰鬥，且莫車年少，百姓未附，恐復危國。

translations.literal: Now pacification not long; people wounded by combat; Qie Moche young in years; hundred surnames not attached; fear again endanger state.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Peace is fresh and the people still scarred by war; Qie Moche is a boy the tribes barely know—you would court fresh disaster.’

sentences.zh: 我與大閼氏一家共子，不如立雕陶莫皋。

translations.literal: I together with Great Chanyu Consort one house shared sons—better establish Diaotaomo Gao.
translations.idiomatic: ‘The senior consort and I are one household with shared sons; better raise Diaotaomo Gao instead.’

sentences.zh: 」大閼氏曰：「且莫車雖少，大臣共持國事，今捨貴立賤，後世必亂。

translations.literal: Great Chanyu Consort said: ‘Though Qie Moche young, great ministers together uphold state affairs; now discard noble establish base—later generations surely chaotic.’
translations.idiomatic: The senior consort objected: ‘Qie Moche is young but the nobles can govern; skipping the senior line for a junior branch invites future strife.’

sentences.zh: 」單于卒從顓渠閼氏計，立雕陶莫皋，約令傳國與弟。

translations.literal: The Chanyu finally followed Zhuqu Chanyu Consort’s plan, established Diaotaomo Gao, covenanted ordering transmit state to younger brother.
translations.idiomatic: Huhanye yielded to Zhuqu, enthroned Diaotaomo Gao, and bound him to pass the realm brother to brother.

sentences.zh: 呼韓邪死，雕陶莫皋立，為復株累若鞮單于。

translations.literal: Huhanye died; Diaotaomo Gao established, became Fuzhulei Ruodi Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: When Huhanye died Diaotaomo Gao succeeded as Chanyu Fuzhulei Ruodi.

sentences.zh: 復株累若鞮單于立，遣子右致盧兒王醯諧屠奴侯入侍，以且糜胥為左賢王，且莫車為左谷蠡王，囊知牙斯為右賢王。

translations.literal: Fuzhulei Ruodi Chanyu established, sent son Right Zhiluer Wang Xi-xie Tunuhou to enter attendance; appointed Qie Mixu as Left Virtuous King, Qie Moche as Left Gu-li King, Nangzhiyasi as Right Virtuous King.
translations.idiomatic: Upon his accession Chanyu Fuzhulei Ruodi sent his son Xi-xie Tunuhou, king of Right Zhiluer, to court as a hostage, named Qie Mixu Left Virtuous king, Qie Moche Left Gu-li king, and Nangzhiyasi Right Virtuous king.

sentences.zh: 復株累單于復妻王昭君，生二女，長女雲為須卜居次，小女為當於居次。

translations.literal: Fuzhulei Chanyu again took wife Wang Zhaojun, bore two daughters; elder daughter Yun was Xubu princess-rank, younger daughter was Dangyu princess-rank.
translations.idiomatic: He took Wang Zhaojun again as consort and fathered two daughters: the elder, Yun, titled Xubu princess, and the younger, titled Dangyu princess.

sentences.zh: 河平元年，單于遣右皋林王伊邪莫演等奉獻朝正月。

translations.literal: First year of Heping, the Chanyu sent Right Gaolin Wang Yexie Moyan and others presenting tribute courting first month.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Heping (28 BCE) the Chanyu dispatched Yexie Moyan, king of Right Gaolin, with tribute for the New Year court.

sentences.zh: 既罷，遣使者送至蒲反。

translations.literal: When finished, sent envoys escort them to Pufan.
translations.idiomatic: After the audience Han envoys escorted the mission as far as Pufan.

sentences.zh: 伊邪莫演言：「欲降，即不受我，我自殺，終不敢還歸。

translations.literal: Yexie Moyan spoke: ‘Wishing to surrender—if then do not accept me, I myself kill—ultimately dare not return.’
translations.idiomatic: Yexie Moyan declared: ‘I mean to defect—refuse me and I shall kill myself; I will never go back north.’

sentences.zh: 」使者以聞，下公卿議。

translations.literal: The envoy thereby reported; handed down to dukes and ministers deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: The escort reported upward and the matter went to the senior ministers.

sentences.zh: 議者或言宜如故事，受其降。

translations.literal: Deliberators some spoke fitting like precedent stories, accept his surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Some urged following precedent and admitting him.

sentences.zh: 光祿大夫谷永、議郎杜欽以為：「漢興，匈奴數為邊害，故設金爵之賞以待降者。

translations.literal: Grandee of Brilliant Virtue Gu Yong and Consultant Du Qin thought: ‘Han arose; the Xiongnu repeatedly became border harm; therefore established gold-rank rewards to await surrenderers.’
translations.idiomatic: Gu Yong, Grandee of Brilliant Virtue, and Consultant Du Qin argued: ‘Early Han faced endless border raids and therefore promised rich rewards for defectors.’

sentences.zh: 今單于詘體稱臣，列為北籓，遣使朝賀，無有二心，漢家接之，宜異於往時。

translations.literal: Now the Chanyu bent body declares subject, arrayed as northern screen, sends envoys court congratulate—without second mind; Han house receives him—fitting differ from former times.
translations.idiomatic: Today the Chanyu bows as vassal, guards our northern marches, and sends punctual embassies—Han should treat him unlike earlier foes.’

sentences.zh: 今既享單于聘貢之質，而更受其逋逃之臣，是貪一夫之得而失一國之心，擁有罪之臣而絕慕義之君也。

translations.literal: Now having already enjoyed the Chanyu’s envoy-tribute pledge, yet further accepting his fleeing fugitive ministers—this is greedy one man’s gain and losing one state’s heart; harboring guilty ministers and cutting off a ruler who admires righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: We already prize his tribute missions; to harbor his runaway noble would trade petty gain for his goodwill and shelter a criminal while alienating a righteous ally.’

sentences.zh: 假令單于初立，欲委身中國，未知利害，私使伊邪莫演詐降以卜吉凶，受之虧德沮善，令單于自疏，不親邊吏；

translations.literal: Suppose the Chanyu newly established, wishing to entrust body Central States, not yet knowing benefit harm; privately ordered Yexie Moyan feign surrender to divine fortune—accepting it injures virtue blocks goodness, ordering the Chanyu himself estranged, not intimate border officials;
translations.idiomatic: Perhaps the new Chanyu probes Han by staging Moyan’s sham defection—accepting it stains our virtue, damps his sincerity, and estranges him from frontier officers.’

sentences.zh: 或者設為反間，欲因而生隙，受之適合其策，使得歸曲而直責。

translations.literal: Or perhaps it is planted counter-espionage meant to open a rift—accepting it fits their plot and lets them paint themselves righteous while faulting us.
translations.idiomatic: Or it may be an outright provocation so we quarrel—taking the bait lets him play injured party and blame us.’

sentences.zh: 此誠邊境安危之原，師旅動靜之首，不可不詳也。

translations.literal: This truly is border peace peril’s root, armies movement rest’s head—cannot but elaborate.
translations.idiomatic: These possibilities strike at frontier stability and troop posture—they deserve the fullest scrutiny.’

sentences.zh: 不如勿受，以昭日月之信，抑詐諼之謀，懷附親之心，便。

translations.literal: Better not accept, thereby display sun-moon’s trustfulness, suppress deceit schemes, cherish attach intimate hearts—convenient.
translations.idiomatic: Better refuse him: we preserve luminous good faith, choke off deceit, and reassure a loyal neighbor—that is the prudent course.’

sentences.zh: 」對奏，天子從之。

translations.literal: The reply was presented; the Son of Heaven followed it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor adopted their advice.

sentences.zh: 遣中郎將王舜往問降狀。

translations.literal: Sent General of the Gentlemen of the Household Wang Shun to go inquire surrender circumstances.
translations.idiomatic: He dispatched Wang Shun, General of the Gentlemen of the Household, to verify the defection.

sentences.zh: 伊邪莫演曰：「我病狂妄言耳。

translations.literal: Yexie Moyan said: ‘I was ill and spoke reckless words, nothing more.’
translations.idiomatic: Yexie Moyan answered: ‘I was feverish and raving.’

sentences.zh: 」遣去。

translations.literal: Ordered him sent away.
translations.idiomatic: They dismissed him.

sentences.zh: 歸到，官位如故，不肯令見漢使。

translations.literal: When Moyan returned home his rank was unchanged, yet the Chanyu forbade him to meet Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Back home he kept his old office, yet the Chanyu barred him from facing Han envoys.

sentences.zh: 明年，單于上書願朝。

translations.literal: The next year, the Chanyu submitted memorial wishing to court.
translations.idiomatic: The following year the Chanyu memorialized his wish to attend court.

sentences.zh: 河平四年正月，遂入朝，加賜錦繡繒帛二萬匹，絮二萬斤，它如竟寧時。

translations.literal: Fourth year of Heping first month, then entered court; additional grants brocade figured silk twenty thousand bolts, floss twenty thousand jin; other like Jingning times.
translations.idiomatic: In the first month of Heping 4 (25 BCE) he arrived for audience and received twenty thousand bolts of figured silk and twenty thousand catties of wadding beyond the usual Jingning-scale gifts.

sentences.zh: 復株累單于立十歲，鴻嘉元年死。

translations.literal: Fuzhulei Chanyu established ten years; died first year of Hongjia.
translations.idiomatic: Chanyu Fuzhulei reigned ten years and died in the first year of Hongjia (20 BCE).

sentences.zh: 弟且糜胥立，為搜諧若鞮單于。

translations.literal: Younger brother Qie Mixu established, became Souxie Ruodi Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Qie Mixu succeeded as Chanyu Souxie Ruodi.

sentences.zh: 搜諧單于立，遣子左祝都韓王朐留斯侯入侍，以且莫車為左賢王。

translations.literal: Souxie Chanyu established, sent son Left Zhudu Hanwang Qu-liusi Hou to enter attendance; appointed Qie Moche as Left Virtuous King.
translations.idiomatic: Chanyu Souxie sent his son Qu-liusi Hou of the Left Zhudu Han king to court as hostage and named Qie Moche Left Virtuous king.

sentences.zh: 搜諧單于立八歲。

translations.literal: Souxie Chanyu established eight years.
translations.idiomatic: Souxie reigned eight years.

sentences.zh: 元延元年，為朝二年發行，未入塞，病死。

translations.literal: First year of Yuanyan, for court second year set out marching; not yet entered passes, sick died.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanyan 1 (12 BCE) he set out for the audience scheduled his second regnal year but died of illness before crossing the frontier.

sentences.zh: 弟且莫車立，為車牙若鞮單于。

translations.literal: Younger brother Qie Moche established, became Cheya Ruodi Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Qie Moche succeeded as Chanyu Cheya Ruodi.

sentences.zh: 車牙單于立，遣子右於塗仇撣王烏夷當入侍，以囊知牙斯為左賢王。

translations.literal: Cheya Chanyu established, sent son Right Yutu Qiudan Wang Wuyidang to enter attendance; appointed Nangzhiyasi as Left Virtuous King.
translations.idiomatic: Cheya sent his son Wuyidang, king of Right Yutu Qiudan, to court and elevated Nangzhiyasi to Left Virtuous king.

sentences.zh: 車牙單于立四歲，綏和元年死。

translations.literal: Cheya Chanyu established four years; died first year of Suihe.
translations.idiomatic: Cheya ruled four years and died in the first year of Suihe (8 BCE).

sentences.zh: 弟囊知牙斯立，為烏珠留若鞮單于。

translations.literal: Younger brother Nangzhiyasi established, became Wuzhuliu Ruodi Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Nangzhiyasi became Chanyu Wuzhuliu Ruodi.

sentences.zh: 烏珠留單于立，以第二閼氏子樂為左賢王，以第五閼氏子輿為右賢王，遣子右股奴王烏鞮牙斯入侍。

translations.literal: Wuzhuliu Chanyu established, appointed second Chanyu Consort’s son Le as Left Virtuous King, fifth Chanyu Consort’s son Yu as Right Virtuous King, sent son Right Gunu Wang Wudiiyasi to enter attendance.
translations.idiomatic: Wuzhuliu named Le, son of his second consort, Left Virtuous king; Yu, son of his fifth consort, Right Virtuous king; and sent Wudiiyasi of the Right Gunu kingship to court.

sentences.zh: 漢遣中郎將夏侯籓、副校尉韓容使匈奴。

translations.literal: Han sent General of the Gentlemen of the Household Xiahou Fan, Deputy Colonel Han Rong as envoys to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Han dispatched Xiahou Fan, General of the Gentlemen of the Household, with Deputy Colonel Han Rong as escort on mission to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 時帝舅大司馬票騎將軍王根領尚書事，或說根曰：「匈奴有斗入漢地，直張掖郡，生奇材木，箭竿就羽，如得之，於邊甚饒，國家有廣地之賣，將軍顯功，垂於無窮。

translations.literal: At the time the emperor’s maternal uncle Grand Marshal General Who Gallops as Swift as a Flying Tiger Wang Gen headed Secretariat affairs; someone persuaded Gen saying: ‘The Xiongnu have a wedge entering Han territory, opposite Zhangye commandery—produces marvelous timber wood; arrow shafts receive feathers—if obtain it, toward border very rich; state family has broaden-lands bargain; general displays merit, hangs to without end.’
translations.idiomatic: The emperor’s uncle Wang Gen headed the Secretariat when an adviser told him: ‘A tongue of nomad land juts toward Zhangye rich in arrow-grade timber—seizing it would fatten the frontier, swell the treasury, and immortalize your name.’

sentences.zh: 」根為上言其利，上直欲從單于求之，為有不得，傷命損威。

translations.literal: Gen spoke of its benefit to the emperor; the emperor wished outright to demand it from the Chanyu—yet fearing refusal would wound the imperial mandate and diminish awe.
translations.idiomatic: Gen pitched the idea at court, but the emperor shrank from an open demand lest refusal shame the throne.

sentences.zh: 根即但以上指曉籓，令從籓所說而求之。

translations.literal: Gen then only by superior intention instructed Fan, ordering follow Fan’s spoken words and seek it.
translations.idiomatic: Gen briefed Fan with the emperor’s gist and told him to pursue it along lines Fan himself devised.

sentences.zh: 籓至匈奴，以語次說單于曰：「竊見匈奴斗入漢地，直張掖郡。

translations.literal: Fan arrived at the Xiongnu; taking occasion in conversation persuaded the Chanyu saying: ‘Privately seeing the Xiongnu wedge entering Han territory, opposite Zhangye commandery.’
translations.idiomatic: In camp Fan remarked in passing: ‘That salient of yours pointing at Zhangye—’

sentences.zh: 漢三都尉居塞上，士卒數百人塞苦，候望久勞。

translations.literal: Han stations three commandants along that stretch of wall; several hundred soldiers endure bitter hardship on the barrier; lookout duty long wearies them.
translations.idiomatic: Han ties up three commandants and hundreds of troops in grinding frontier watch along that wedge.’

sentences.zh: 單于宜上書獻此地，直斷閼之，省兩都尉士卒數百人，以復天子厚恩，其報必大。

translations.literal: The Chanyu fittingly submits a memorial offering this land; straight cut and block it off; saves two commandants’ soldiers several hundred men; thereby repaying the Son of Heaven’s thick grace—the recompense surely great.
translations.idiomatic: Memorialize its cession and Han can seal the salient, retire two commands, spare hundreds of men, and still earn lavish gratitude from the throne.’

sentences.zh: 」單于曰：「此天子詔語邪，將從使者所求也？

translations.literal: The Chanyu said: ‘Is this Son of Heaven edict speech, or will follow what the envoy seeks?’
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu asked: ‘Are these the emperor’s own words, or merely your proposal?’

sentences.zh: 」籓曰：「詔指也，然籓亦為單于畫善計耳。

translations.literal: Fan said: ‘It is edict intention; yet Fan also for the Chanyu draws good plan.’
translations.idiomatic: Fan replied: ‘It reflects imperial intent, though I phrased it as friendly counsel.’

sentences.zh: 」單于曰：「孝宣、孝元皇帝哀憐父呼韓邪單于，從長城以北匈奴有之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu said: ‘Emperors Xiaoxuan and Xiaoyuan pitied father Chanyu Huhanye; from Great Wall northward the Xiongnu hold it.’
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu answered: ‘Emperors Xuan and Yuan pitied my father Huhanye—everything north of the Wall belongs to us.’

sentences.zh: 此溫偶駼王所居地也，未曉其形狀所生，請遣使問之。

translations.literal: This is land where King Wen’outu dwells; not yet understand its shape appearance produced—request send envoy inquire it.
translations.idiomatic: That wedge is Wen’outu’s territory; I must learn its exact bounds—allow me to consult him through envoys.’

sentences.zh: 」籓、容歸漢。

translations.literal: Fan and Rong returned to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Fan and Han Rong departed for the capital.

sentences.zh: 後復使匈奴，至則求地。

translations.literal: Later again sent to Xiongnu; arriving then sought land.
translations.idiomatic: On a later mission they pressed for the cession as soon as they arrived.

sentences.zh: 單于曰：「父兄傳五世，漢不求此地，至知獨求，何也？

translations.literal: The Chanyu said: ‘Fathers and elder brothers transmitted five generations—Han did not seek this land; until now alone seeks—why?’
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu protested: ‘Five reigns of fathers and brothers held this soil without complaint from Han—why does the court demand it only now?’

sentences.zh: 已問溫偶駼王，匈奴西邊諸侯作穹廬及車，皆仰此山材木，且先父地，不敢失也。

translations.literal: Already questioned King Wen’outu—the Xiongnu western marches’ nobles make yurts and carts; all rely on this mountain’s timber; moreover it is former fathers’ land—dare not lose it.
translations.idiomatic: I asked Wen’outu: every western chief builds carts and tents from that ridge—it was my fathers’ pasture and cannot be surrendered.’

sentences.zh: 」籓還，遷為太原太守。

translations.literal: Fan returned; transferred to be Taiyuan governor.
translations.idiomatic: Fan went home promoted governor of Taiyuan.

sentences.zh: 單于遣使上書，以籓求地狀聞。

translations.literal: The Chanyu sent envoys submitting memorial, reporting Fan’s seeking-land circumstances.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu memorialized the court detailing Fan’s land grab.

sentences.zh: 詔報單于曰：「籓擅稱詔從單于求地，法當死，更大赦二，今徙籓為濟南太守，不令當匈奴。

translations.literal: An edict replied to the Chanyu saying: ‘Fan presumptuously claimed edict asking land from the Chanyu—by law deserves death; encountered two great amnesties; now transfer Fan to be Jinan governor, not order him face the Xiongnu.’
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered: ‘Fan forged imperial authority to extort you—a capital crime twice remitted by general amnesty; he is banished to Jinan and barred from frontier duty.’

sentences.zh: 」明年，侍子死，歸葬。

translations.literal: The next year the hostage son died; returned for burial.
translations.idiomatic: The following year the hostage prince died and his body was sent home.

sentences.zh: 復遣子左於駼仇撣王稽留昆入侍。

translations.literal: Again sent son Left Yutu Qiudan Wang Jiliukun to enter attendance.
translations.idiomatic: He sent another son, Jiliukun, king of Left Yutu Qiudan, to court.

sentences.zh: 至哀帝建平二年，烏孫庶子卑援疐翕侯人眾入匈奴西界，寇盜牛畜，頗殺其民。

translations.literal: Reaching Emperor Ai’s Jianping second year, Wusun secondary son Beiyuanzhi Xi-hou’s people multitudes entered Xiongnu western border, raided stole cattle and livestock, somewhat killed their people.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianping 2 (5 BCE) Beiyuanzhi of Wusun led his Xi-hou band across the western frontier to rustle herds and kill locals.

sentences.zh: 單于聞之，遣左大當戶烏夷泠將五千騎擊烏孫，殺數百八，略千餘人，驅牛畜去。

translations.literal: The Chanyu hearing it, sent Left Grand Household Wuyiling leading five thousand riders to strike Wusun, killed several hundred persons, plundered over a thousand people, drove cattle and livestock away.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu sent Wuyiling at the head of five thousand horsemen; they slew hundreds, carried off more than a thousand captives, and drove away the herds.

sentences.zh: 卑援疐恐，遣子趨逯為質匈奴。

translations.literal: Beiyuanzhi feared, sent son Qulu as hostage to Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Beiyuanzhi panicked and gave his son Qulu as a hostage to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 單于受，以狀聞。

translations.literal: The Chanyu accepted; reported circumstances.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu accepted the prince and memorialized the fact.

sentences.zh: 漢遣中郎將丁野林、副校尉公乘音使匈奴，責讓單于，告令還歸卑援疐質子。

translations.literal: Han sent General of the Gentlemen of the Household Ding Yelin, Deputy Colonel Gongsheng Yin as envoys to Xiongnu, reproached the Chanyu, ordered return Beiyuanzhi’s hostage son.
translations.idiomatic: Han dispatched Ding Yelin and Deputy Colonel Gongsheng Yin to rebuke the Chanyu and demand Qulu’s return.

sentences.zh: 單于受詔，遣歸。

translations.literal: The Chanyu received edict; sent him back.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu obeyed and sent the boy home.

sentences.zh: 建平四年，單于上書願朝五年。

translations.literal: Jianping fourth year, the Chanyu submitted memorial wishing to court in the fifth year.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianping 4 (3 BCE) he memorialized his wish to attend court in the fifth regnal year.

sentences.zh: 時哀帝被疾，或言匈奴從上游來厭人，自黃龍、竟寧時，單于朝中國輒有大故。

translations.literal: At the time Emperor Ai was afflicted ill; some spoke that Xiongnu coming from upper stream presses down people; since Huanglong and Jingning times, when Chanyu courted Central States thereupon there were great calamities.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ai lay sick when rumor claimed Xiongnu embassies brought ill omens—each visit since Huanglong and Jingning had coincided with imperial deaths.

sentences.zh: 上由是難之，以問公卿，亦以為虛費府帑，可且勿許。

translations.literal: The emperor thereby found it difficult; asked dukes and ministers—they also thought empty expense treasury funds; could temporarily not permit.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor hesitated and polled his ministers, who cited cost and urged refusal.

sentences.zh: 單于使辭去，未發，黃門郎揚雄上書諫曰：

translations.literal: The Chanyu’s envoy took leave to depart; not yet set out; Gentlemen of the Yellow Gate Yang Xiong submitted memorial remonstrating saying:
translations.idiomatic: As the embassy prepared to leave, Yang Xiong of the Yellow Gate memorialized:

sentences.zh: 臣聞《六經》之治，貴於未亂；

translations.literal: Your servant hears that in governing by the Six Classics, esteem lies before chaos;
translations.idiomatic: The Classics teach that rule excels when disorder has not yet stirred;

sentences.zh: 兵家之勝，貴於未戰。

translations.literal: In military masters’ victory, esteem lies before battle.
translations.idiomatic: the art of war prizes triumph won without crossing blades.

sentences.zh: 二者皆微，然而大事之本，不可不察也。

translations.literal: Both matters are subtle; yet they are great affairs’ root—cannot but examine.
translations.idiomatic: Both truths sound slight yet underpin every great decision—we neglect them at peril.

sentences.zh: 今單于上書求朝，國家不許而辭之，臣愚以為漢與匈奴從此隙矣。

translations.literal: Now the Chanyu submits memorial seeking court; state does not permit but dismisses him—your servant foolishly thinks Han and the Xiongnu from this crack.
translations.idiomatic: Today the Chanyu asks to attend and we send him away—I fear Han and the Xiongnu part here with a lasting grudge.

sentences.zh: 本北地之狄，五帝所不能臣，三王所不能制，其不可使隙甚明。

translations.literal: Originally northern lands’ Di—the Five Emperors could not make subjects; Three Kings could not control—its must-not-allow crack very clear.
translations.idiomatic: They are the northern Di whom neither the Five Emperors nor the Three Dynasties fully subdued—we must not invite their enmity.

sentences.zh: 臣不敢遠稱，請引秦以來明之。

translations.literal: Your servant dares not cite distant examples; please draw Qin onward to clarify.
translations.idiomatic: I need not reach deep antiquity—let Qin and Han speak.

sentences.zh: 以秦始皇之強，蒙恬之威，帶甲四十餘萬，然不敢窺西河，乃築長城以界之。

translations.literal: With Qin Shihuang’s strength, Meng Tian’s awesomeness, belt-armor more than four hundred thousand—yet did not dare peer westward beyond the River; then built Great Wall to bound them.
translations.idiomatic: Even Qin Shihuang and Meng Tian with forty thousand armored troops dared not cross the Yellow River westward—they raised the Long Wall as a boundary.

sentences.zh: 會漢初興，以高祖之威靈，三十萬眾困於平城，士或七日不食。

translations.literal: When Han first arose, with Gaozu’s numinous awe, three hundred thousand masses trapped at Pingcheng; soldiers some seven days without eating.
translations.idiomatic: At Han’s founding Gaozu himself led three hundred thousand men into the Pingcheng trap where some starved seven days.

sentences.zh: 時奇譎之士石畫之臣甚眾，卒其所以脫者，世莫得而言也。

translations.literal: At the time crafty stratagem knights stone-planning ministers very many; ultimately the means by which they escaped—generations cannot speak it.
translations.idiomatic: Clever advisers thronged the court, yet how the emperor escaped remains untold.

sentences.zh: 又高皇后嘗忿匈奴，群臣庭議，樊噲請以十萬眾橫行匈奴中，季布曰：「噲可斬也，妄阿順指！

translations.literal: Moreover Empress Gao once resented Xiongnu; assembled ministers court deliberated; Fan Kuai requested with hundred thousand masses rampage within Xiongnu; Ji Bu said: ‘Kuai can be beheaded—recklessly toadying compliant aims!’
translations.idiomatic: When Empress Lü raged at the Xiongnu, Fan Kuai vowed to sweep the steppe with a hundred thousand men until Ji Bu demanded his head for reckless flattery.’

sentences.zh: 」於是大臣權書遺之，然後匈奴之結解，中國之憂平。

translations.literal: Thereupon great ministers by expedient letter sent to them; afterward Xiongnu’s knot dissolved; Central States’ worry pacified.
